description: Trigger and behavior cases for API/event contract design: credential-derived tenant context, honest delivery semantics, versioning with sunset windows, signed webhooks.

cases.prompt: We're opening a public API and webhooks so customers can integrate. Design the contracts before the first partner builds against us.

assertions: Derives tenant context from the credential and forbids client-supplied tenant ids on data paths.
Defines idempotency for mutations, per-tenant and plan-derived rate limits, and a 404-vs-403 policy that leaks no tenant existence.
Specifies a versioned event envelope with thin payloads, tenant-scoped subscriptions, at-least-once delivery with a documented retry schedule, and signed, replay-protected webhooks.
Includes a versioning/deprecation policy with a dual-run window and minimum notice, plus the consumer checklist as part of the contract.

cases.prompt: An agency partner manages 30 of our customer workspaces and wants one API token and one webhook endpoint for all of them. How should that work?

assertions: Designs the aggregator as an explicit allow-list bound to the credential, granted and revocable per tenant — never a wildcard.
Keeps every event and API response scoped to a named tenant in the envelope, with per-call tenant selection validated against the allow-list.
Flags the cross-tenant credential as a designed exception with negative tests for reaching a non-granted tenant.

cases.prompt: Compliance needs a durable internal record of every admin action and export, with tamper evidence.

assertions: A tamper-evident internal record is audit-log-architect's job; nothing here is an integration-facing contract.

cases.prompt: Rename the 'customer_ref' field to 'client_id' in the API responses next release — partners will just have to update.

assertions: Identifies the rename as a breaking change to a live contract and refuses the flag-day framing.
Establishes the deprecation sequence (announce, dual-run with both fields, warn, enforce) and routes the change through human-approval-boundary with the affected-consumer count.
